text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
St Thomas' Hospital  
Neurosciences Department, Westminster Bridge Road, London SE1 7EH  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Emily Dawson, DOB: 14-11-1990, Hospital No: R928417 NHS No. 7401563928  
58 Alder Grove, London, SW8 1JP  

Diagnoses: 

Symptomatic generalised epilepsy. 

I reviewed Emily Dawson in clinic today. She attends alone and reports a notable deterioration in her seizure control over the past 4–5 months. Historically, she has generalised epilepsy and she has a past history of atonic seizures, drop attacks and generalised tonic–clonic seizures one to two times per year, but seizure control has been excellent, and she has now been seizure free for several years. However, since increasing work pressures and longer hours, she has experienced clusters of jumps almost daily, particularly on workdays, consistent with her report that high workload has been a precipitating factor. She describes these as brief, sudden axial and upper limb jerks with occasional object dropping; she also notes intermittent disequilibrium and near‑falls. There have been two definite drop events in the last six weeks. No generalised tonic–clonic seizures have been witnessed, but her partner reports brief staring and word‑finding pauses on two occasions. 

She links the worsening to work‑related stress and poor sleep. Events tend to cluster on workdays per patient report, with relative improvement on weekends. There is no recent intercurrent illness, alcohol excess, or new medications. She denies missed anti‑seizure medication, though acknowledges variable timing on late shifts. Menses regular; no pregnancy. 

Current treatment: lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily; levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily. She has tolerated these without rash or mood lability; mild irritability attributed to work fatigue. She is not using rescue medication currently. No vagus nerve stimulator in situ. 

Examination today was unremarkable: alert, oriented, speech fluent; cranial nerves intact; no focal motor deficit; gait steady in clinic though she describes occasional morning unsteadiness. Blood pressure 118/72, pulse 72 regular. 

Given the deterioration and daily clustering of myoclonic‑type events, I have discussed seizure safety at work (avoidance of ladders, hot surfaces; shower rather than bath; supervision with heavy machinery) and driving regulations. She does not currently drive. We agreed to obtain updated bloods (U&Es, LFTs, FBC, vitamin D) and anti‑seizure medication levels where available, and to arrange an EEG, including activation procedures, to look for generalised spike‑wave or polyspike‑wave activity. A brain MRI from 2018 was normal; we will repeat MRI if clinically indicated after EEG review. 

She will start a structured sleep routine and stress‑reduction plan (patient‑led), and keep a seizure diary noting triggers, timing, and any clusters. We also discussed the option of dose optimisation if events persist; plan to consider a cautious lamotrigine uptitration or adjunctive valproate discussion if appropriate and acceptable in future. She understands this is an interim plan while we complete investigations. 

In summary, despite several years of excellent control, there is clear worsening temporally associated with occupational stress: she has experienced clusters of jumps almost daily, with occasional drops and brief absences suggested. This represents a deterioration from her prior baseline of rare events. I have asked her to contact the service urgently if she has any generalised tonic–clonic seizures, injuries, or any prolonged clusters. 

Follow‑up in 6–8 weeks after EEG, or sooner if needed. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: she has experienced clusters of jumps almost daily
she has experienced clusters of jumps almost daily,

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis:
- Identify current seizure activity mentioned: "clusters of jumps almost daily" (myoclonic-type events), two drop events in the last six weeks, and two brief staring episodes reported without a precise timeframe.
- Apply highest-frequency rule: "almost daily" clusters represent the most frequent events compared to two drops in six weeks (~0.33/week) and two brief staring episodes (timeframe unclear).
- Normalize wording: "almost daily" is approximated to a daily frequency. Since the exact number per cluster is not provided, and to avoid the cluster format requiring per-cluster counts, use a standard per-day label. Thus, select "1 per day" as the closest normalized form to "almost daily".
- Quote chosen directly reflects the frequency statement.
seizure_frequency_number: she has experienced clusters of jumps almost daily
reference: she has experienced clusters of jumps almost daily,
